name: FTC/TAF preprint reported DNAm aging decreases while FTC/TDF did not
summary: The FTC/TAF preprint adds a human biological-age biomarker signal for reverse transcriptase inhibition, but it is post hoc, young-adult, preprint evidence without direct transposable-element target engagement.
statement: In a medRxiv post hoc analysis of healthy adults without HIV, FTC/TAF for 12 weeks was associated with decreases in DNA methylation aging measures and epigenetic inflammatory proxies, while a separate FTC/TDF study did not show significant epigenetic-clock changes.
population_or_model: Healthy adults without HIV aged 18 to 50 years
quantitative_note: FTC/TAF N=36: DunedinPACE -0.061, p=0.019; PhenoAge -6.33 years, p=0.008; epigenetic IL-6 -0.058, p=0.029. FTC/TDF N=43: no significant changes across epigenetic clocks and proxies.
caveats: The source is a preprint and should be rechecked for peer review or correction.
The analysis was post hoc and used samples from pharmacokinetic studies rather than a prespecified geroscience efficacy trial.
The population was young and healthy, not older adults or an aging-intervention cohort.
The reported endpoints are DNA methylation clocks and epigenetic inflammatory proxies, not direct LINE1, retrotransposon cDNA, cGAS-STING, interferon, functional, disease, or mortality outcomes.
The article reports institutional research-contract and scientific-advisory conflicts involving antiretroviral manufacturers for some authors.
